Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss Or Stay Action And Compel Arbitration Of Shaddocks Claims Against Laguna
Off Calendar Jun-27-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Atty. Robert Weiss of the Hendricks Law, Tel. No. 1-415-829-4221 appearing
for the Plaintiff;
Atty. Alan Bishop of Hanson Bridgett, Tel. No. 1-415-777-3200 appearing for
the Defendants.

RULING - A R G U E D; DEFENDANTS SCOTT KAY, DEBORAH KAY AND 1833 LAGUNA LLC'S Motion To Dismiss Or Stay Action And Compel Arbitration Of Shaddocks Claims Against Laguna is taken under submission. Judge: Richard Ulmer; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Robert Balian, CSR #5220 =302/ru

* * * After hearing, the court signed an order and ruled as follows: DEFENDANTS SCOTT KAY, DEBORAH KAY and 1833 LAGUNA LLC'S Motion To Dismiss Or Stay Action And Compel Arbitration Of Shaddocks Claims Against Laguna - In a dispute over a construction renovation project, defendant 1833 Laguna LLC moves to arbitrate five of the causes of action at issue and to dismiss or stay this court action. In their construction contract's arbitration agreement, the parties stipulated that "this agreement to arbitrate shall be governed by the Federal Arbitration Act." A trial court's "only option in these circumstances is to stay the court proceeding and compel the arbitration." (Rodriguez v. American Technologies, Inc. (2006) 136 Cal.App.4th 1110, 1115.) As here, the party opposing arbitration in Rodriguez tried to rely on Code of Civil Procedure 1281.2(c), but the Court of Appeal ruled that the FAA's procedural provisions control over state law. (136 Cal.App.4th at 1115, 1122.) Plaintiff points to a statement in another part of the construction contract: "this Agreement shall be governed by the law in effect at the location of the Project" - i.e., California law. However, this provision is outside the parties' agreement to arbitrate and does not mention arbitration; it clearly refers to substantive, not procedural, law. (See Civil Code 1641 ("whole of contract is to be taken together, so as to give effect to every part").) "Thus, there is no ambiguity regarding the parties' intent. They adopted the FAA - all of it - to govern their arbitration." (Rodriguez, 136 Cal.App.4th at 1122.) The motion to stay this court action and to compel arbitration is GRANTED. * * *

Notice Of Joinder And Joinder In Defendant 1833 Laguna Llcs Motion To Compel Arbitration (Joinder)

A trial court's "only option in these circumstances is to stay the court proceeding and compel the arbitration." (Rodriguez v. American Technologies, Inc. (2006) 136 Cal.App.4th 1110, 1115.) As here, the party opposing arbitration in Rodriguez tried to rely on Code of Civil Procedure 1281.2(c), but the Court of Appeal ruled that the FAA's procedural provisions control over state law. (138 Cal.App.4th at 1115, 1122.) Plaintiff points to a statement in another part of the construction contract: "this Agreement shall be governed by the law in effect at the location of the Project" - i.e., California law. However, this provision is outside the parties' agreement to arbitrate and does not mention arbitration; it clearly refers to substantive, not procedural, law. (See Civil Code 1641 ("whole of contract is to be taken together, so as to give effect to every part").) "Thus, there is no ambiguity regarding the parties' intent. They adopted the FAA - all of it - to govern their arbitration." (Rodriguez, 136 Cal.App.4th at 1122.) The motion to stay this court action and to compel arbitration is GRANTED. * * *
